coredata.dc:title: The synergistic cooperation of carbon quantum dots and Fe MOF for the greatly improved charge separation efficiency of MW:BVO
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Lei
Wang, Lin
Chu, Shuai
Zhai, Wei
Li, Jie
Li, Xiaoru
Jiao, Zhengbo
coredata.dc:description: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is a promising photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting material. However, its high carrier recombination rate, slow surface reaction kinetics, and narrow light absorption range have been major bottleneck problems. In this work, a novel composite material, MW:BVO@NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (Fe MOF)@CQDs, was successfully synthesized by simultaneous modification of the MW:BVO(BVO co-doped with Mo and W) surface with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Fe MOF nanoparticles. The introduction of Fe MOF and CQDs as co-catalysts significantly improved the photocurrent density of MW:BVO by nearly 5 times. CQDs, as a non-toxic and safe green material, exhibit excellent electron transfer ability and up-conversion luminescence characteristics, which help to suppress the recombination of photo-generated carriers and broaden the light absorption spectrum. The incorporation of CQDs resulted in a significant increase in the photocurrent density of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs up to 5.9 mA cm−2, while enhancing its light absorption ability. This enhancement can be attributed to two factors: firstly, the introduction of Ov defects due to the co-catalysis of CQDs and Fe MOF can greatly improve the OER reaction kinetics; secondly, the introduction of Fe MOF and CQDs can provide more active sites for oxygen evolution, and the synergistic effect of the dual co-catalysts promotes the separation of photogenerated carrier pairs, which may account for the significant improvement in the PEC performance of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs. Therefore, this work provides some interesting new insights for the construction of efficient photocatalytic anodes for PEC applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MW:BVO photoanode
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Metal-organic framework
Photoelectrochemical

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2019MB006), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M632610, 2017M610409).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of MW:BVO, MW:BVO@Fe MOF and MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanodes.
(a) Schematic of the synthesis of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanodes. SEM images of (b) Fe MOF. (c) MW:BVO@Fe MOF. (d) MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs and (e) mapping of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode.
TEM images of (a-c) MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs. (d, e) MW:BVO and (f) EDX of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs.
XPS spectra of Bi 4 f (a), V 2p (b), O 1 s (c) and C 1 s (d) of MW:BVO and MW:BVO-based photoanodes.
(a) LSV curves of MW:BVO and MW:BVO-based electrodes under light illumination. (b) PL curves. (c) LSV curves with Na2SO3 solution. (d) I-t curves.
(a) UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra. (b) The band gap energy of MW:BVO and MW:BVO-based photoanodes.
(a) surface charge injection efficiency. (b) LSV curves of electrodes without illumination; (c) ABPE curves. (d) IPCE curves. (e) APCE curves. (f) LHE curves.

(a) I-t stability test at 1.23 V vs. RHE during 30 min illumination. (b) Open-circuit potential under darkness. (c) EIS curves of MW:BVO and MW:BVO-based photonanodes.(d) Anodic transient dynamics under AM 1.5 G solar light. (e) Schematic of the calculation of the normalized parameter D in the transient kinetics. (f) Transient photocurrent responses at 1.23 V vs. RHE.
Schematic diagram of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode for PEC water oxidation.
A summary of hydrogen produnction of BiVO4 photoanodes with various modifications.

entry.bold: 1 M phosphate buffer solution with pH of 7.4.

head.ce:title: The synergistic cooperation of carbon quantum dots and Fe MOF for the greatly improved charge separation efficiency of MW:BVO

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials for Energy and Environment, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials for Energy and Environment, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materials for Energy and Environment, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is a promising photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting material. However, its high carrier recombination rate, slow surface reaction kinetics, and narrow light absorption range have been major bottleneck problems. In this work, a novel composite material, MW:BVO@NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (Fe MOF)@CQDs, was successfully synthesized by simultaneous modification of the MW:BVO(BVO co-doped with Mo and W) surface with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Fe MOF nanoparticles. The introduction of Fe MOF and CQDs as co-catalysts significantly improved the photocurrent density of MW:BVO by nearly 5 times. CQDs, as a non-toxic and safe green material, exhibit excellent electron transfer ability and up-conversion luminescence characteristics, which help to suppress the recombination of photo-generated carriers and broaden the light absorption spectrum. The incorporation of CQDs resulted in a significant increase in the photocurrent density of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs up to 5.9 mA cm−2, while enhancing its light absorption ability. This enhancement can be attributed to two factors: firstly, the introduction of Ov defects due to the co-catalysis of CQDs and Fe MOF can greatly improve the OER reaction kinetics; secondly, the introduction of Fe MOF and CQDs can provide more active sites for oxygen evolution, and the synergistic effect of the dual co-catalysts promotes the separation of photogenerated carrier pairs, which may account for the significant improvement in the PEC performance of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs. Therefore, this work provides some interesting new insights for the construction of efficient photocatalytic anodes for PEC applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The present study proposes a novel strategy to improve the performance of BiVO4 photoanodes by incorporating a dual-layered co-catalyst system comprising Fe MOF and CQDs. The inclusion of Fe MOF and CQDs coatings enhances the activity sites for oxygen evolution, thereby augmenting the water oxidation activity and photoelectrochemical stability. Compared to the pristine BiVO4 photoanode, the modified Mo,W:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs composite photoanode exhibits a remarkable 6-fold increase in photocurrent density. The dual-layered co-catalyst modification surpasses the performance obtained by solely employing Fe MOF modification, providing higher photocurrent density and stronger photoelectrochemical performance. This study highlights the immense potential of dual-layered co-catalysts in advancing the development of traditional PEC photoanodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of industry and technology has led to problems such as environmental pollution and energy shortage. These problems have become obstacles to the sustainable development of human society, which has attracted global attention [1–3]. The current research believes that the development of photochemical (PEC) water decomposition technology is a forward-looking strategy to solve the energy crisis, which can greatly reduce the dependence on traditional fossil fuels. From the perspective of meeting the green hydrogen production, the most promising approach is to establish efficient photocatalytic technology to achieve the purpose of preparing hydrogen, which has attracted extensive attention from researchers. BiVO4 is a promising metal-oxide-semiconductor photoanode material, which has attracted much attention due to its superior visible light absorption capacity, suitable band gap (∼ 2.4 eV), high photochemical activity and stability [4,5]. However, the short electron transport distance (∼70 nm), slow water oxidation kinetics, and short carrier transport (∼ 4.4×10−2 cm−2 V−1 s−1), The photodegradation of the BiVO4 photoanode results in a photocurrent density below the theoretical limit (7.5 mA cm−2 (100 mW cm−2)) [6–8]. Therefore, in order to enhance the electron transfer of BiVO4, various strategies have been employed, including the introduction of oxygen vacancies, metal doping, and the construction of heterojunctions [9,10]. In addition, lattice defects inherent in BiVO4, such as Bi3+ and V5+, form defect sites. In order to solve this problem, a large number of studies have been conducted and various improvement strategies have been explored. For example, by introducing foreign atoms such as Mo6+ and W6+ to replace V5+ in the BiVO4 lattice by doping, the carrier density and conductivity of BiVO4 can be increased, and the carrier transfer dynamics can be improved. However, these traditional methods may introduce defects or charge traps where hole recombination may be more pronounced [11,12].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have garnered significant research attention in recent years, particularly as synergistic catalysts for improving photocatalytic performance, owing to their abundant metal ions serving as active catalytic centers, high surface area, unique porous structure, and adjustable functional groups [13,14]. These highly porous and multifunctional nanomaterials exhibit important characteristics such as high porosity, large surface area, diverse structural functionalities, and unsaturated metal sites, which make them potent adsorbents and catalysts [15,16]. MOFs and their derivatives are commonly used as photocatalysts or electrode materials for energy conversion and storage[17]. Among these derivatives, Fe, Co, and Ni-based MOFs have been employed as synergistic catalysts for BiVO4 [18–20]. In particular, Fe-MOF serves as a shielding layer and synergistic catalyst for oxygen-rich BiVO4, effectively preserving oxygen vacancies and enhancing the photocurrent and stability of the photoanode [21,22]. NH2-MIL-101(Fe) is considered a leading MOF material due to its superior stability in aqueous environments, extensive metal active sites, and cost-effectiveness [23–25]. Consequently, the Fe MOF@MW:BVO composite material appears to be a highly promising photocatalyst candidate [26,27].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of carbon material, have been paid more attention in the field of new materials research because of their simple preparation method and photoluminescence. Therefore, in order to improve the photocatalytic activity of the semiconductor in the visible light range, the researchers used CQDs to modify the semiconductor to improve the photocatalytic performance [28,29]. Furthermore, the conjugated π structure and abundant surface functional groups of CQDs provide the necessary conditions for their assembly with other semiconductors [30]. By constructing semiconductor/CQDs composite materials, CQDs can be used as electron media to enhance the photocatalytic activity of catalysts [31,32]. For instance, the presence of CQD carriers can significantly optimize various parameters such as surface area, charge carrier transport pathways and electrolyte contact in io-BiVO4 [33]. Moreover, research demonstrates that negatively charged CQDs particles and Fe-MOF substrates can form dynamic heterojunctions, which promote electron-hole separation during the PEC process [34–36]. These findings demonstrate the exceptional synergistic effect between CQDs and MW: BVO, NM-101 (Fe), pave the way for their further applications in photocatalytic water splitting [37].

This research investigates the defects in BiVO4, which weaken electron-hole recombination, accelerate charge transfer, and affect surface water oxidation kinetics [38,39]. To address this issue, we developed a novel modification strategy using a double layer of Fe MOF and CQDs as auxiliary catalysts ( Fig. 1(a)) [40]. This approach increases the number of reaction oxidation sites and effectively improves the water oxidation activity and photoelectrochemical stability [41]. After CQDs modification of Fe MOF@MW:BVO, the increase in oxidation density significantly improves the oxygen evolution reaction kinetics [42,43]. Furthermore, at the band structure level, CQDs greatly enhance UV–vis absorption and further improve the light harvesting efficiency of BiVO4 [44]. In conclusion, MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs demonstrates excellent photoelectrochemical performance, with 5 and 2.98 times photoelectric current increase, respectively, compared to unmodified MW:BVO and MW:BVO@Fe MOF, at 1.23 VRHE [45].

NH2-MIL-101(Fe)(Fe MOF) was synthesized via a hydrothermal approach. Briefly, 1.62 g of FeCl3 · 6 H2O was subjected to sonication in 72 mL of Dimethylformamide (DMF), after which 0.54 g of (NH2-BDC) was added. The resulting solution was transferred to a 120 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 110 °C for 20 h. Following cooling, the precipitate was isolated by centrifugation, washed thrice with DMF and ethanol, and then dried at 150 °C for 8 h. Consequently, dark brown Fe MOF solid materials were obtained.

The present study used an advanced drying and calcination method to prepare nanoporous BiVO4. Firstly, bismuth nitride (Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O) and ammonium vanadate (NH4VO3) were dissolved in 75 mM ethylene glycol separately. Then, 0.34 g of polyethylene glycol (PEG-600) was dissolved in 10 mL of ethylene glycol. By mixing these three solutions, BiVO4 precursor solution was obtained. Finally, the precursor solution was dropped onto a FTO substrate and was first dried at 130 °C for 1 h and then calcined at 500 °C for 2.5 h to prepare BiVO4 samples.

The MW:BVO was synthesized using a similar procedure. Initially, 6 mg of (NH4)10H2(W2O7)6 and 4 mg of (NH4)6Mo7O24 were introduced into an ammonium vanadate (NH4VO3) solution. The subsequent steps remained unchanged. 2.1.3. Preparation of MW:BVO@Fe MOF

The preparation process of MW:BVO@Fe MOF composite materials involves several steps. Initially, Fe MOF powder is ground to achieve uniform particle size. Subsequently, 1 mg of the Fe MOF powder is dissolved in 1 mL of ethylene glycol and subjected to 30 min of ultrasonication. Following this, the MW:BVO surface was spin-coated with 100 µL of the prepared Fe MOF solution to ensure uniform coverage. The MW:BVO@Fe MOF photoanodes were then baked at 100 °C for 1 h. For detailed information regarding the reagents and materials used, please refer to the Supporting Informaiton.

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized via an alkali-assisted ultrasonic treatment method. Firstly, 9 g of glucose was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of an equal volume of NaOH solution (1 M) to the glucose solution, and the mixture was subjected to ultrasonic treatment at room temperature for 6 h to synthesize carbon quantum dots. The pH of the mixture should be adjusted to 7 by adding drops of hydrogen chloride, followed by the addition of 50 mL of ethanol with continuous agitation.

To prepare MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs, 200 µL of the CQDs solution was taken and spin-coated onto MW:BVO@ Fe MOF. The resultant mixture was then baked at 100 °C for 1 h. In order to clearly distinguish between various photoanodes, the following naming protocol was employed: MW:BVO@Fe MOF(1)@CQDs(1), MW:BVO@Fe MOF(2)@CQDs(1), and MW:BVO@Fe MOF (1)@CQDs(2) were assigned to photoanodes containing different quantities of CQDs and Fe MOF. Henceforth, all MW:BVO@Fe MOF(1)@CQDs(2) photoanodes will be referred to collectively as MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs.

The photoanode phases were assessed by means of a Rigaku Ultima IV device that employed Cu Kα radiation (40 kW), featuring a range from 10° to 90° with a scan rate of 0.0678 s−1. Surface morphology and microstructures were characterized using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JSM-7800 F. JEOL) operated at an accelerating voltage of 5 kW. Further characterization was conducted through the use of a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100 Plus), with high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements obtained. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained via the PHI 5000 Versa Probe III, with argon-ion-cluster-etched XPS spectra acquired through continuous etching with an argon-ion beam. UV-Vis spectrophotometry (UV-2700, Shimadzu) was utilized to generate the light absorption spectra.

To assess the photoelectrochemical properties of the materials, a standard three-electrode system was employed. The CH Instrument CHI 660E electrochemical workstation served as the apparatus, with the Pt wire mesh deployed as the counter electrode and the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) used as the reference electrode. The 0.1 M Na2SO4 phosphate buffer solution was utilized as the electrolyte, with a neutral pH value of 7.4. For test light source purposes, a 300 W xenon lamp was utilized, featuring an AM 1.5 G filter (HSX-F300, Beijing NBeT Technology Co., Ltd.). Using a CEL-NP2000 radiometer from Beijing Au-light Co., Ltd. the optical power density of the light source was calibrated to 100 mW cm−2.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was employed to analyze the crystalline structures of the photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 2a and Fig. S1, it was observed that highly pure MW:BVO and Fe MOF were successfully synthesized, with no impurity peaks observed compared to standard cards and literature [46,48]. The good match of characteristic peaks at 19.0°, 28.9°, 30.5°, 35.2°, 39.8°, 42.5°, 53.3°, and 47.3° indicates a good match with standard monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 14–0688) [49]. It is noteworthy that the structural integrity of MW:BVO was maintained after Fe MOF and CQDs coating. No additional diffraction peaks of Fe MOF and CQDs were observed in the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode. However, a significant coating was observed on the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode, indicating a low loading of Fe MOF and CQDs on the surface of MW:BVO [50,51]. This coating demonstrates the close contact between Fe MOF, CQDs, and MW:BVO. Furthermore, after adding different levels of co-catalysts or changing the coating sequence, no significant differences were observed in the XRD pattern, further supporting the low loading of Fe MOF and CQDs on the surface of BiVO4 [52].

The synthesis process of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode is depicted in Fig. 2a. Fe MOF and CQDs were sequentially spin-coated onto the MW:BVO photoanode, denoted as MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs. MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanodes were characterized for their morphology and composition using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The SEM image in Fig. S2 reveals the uniform dispersion of approximately 200 nm irregular BiVO4 particles on FTO glass substrate. The Fe MOF particles exhibit regular sponge-like octahedral morphology, as revealed by images in Fig. 2b, with a mean height of 800 nm and a side length of 400 nm. As shown in Fig. 2c, effective deposition of Fe MOF onto the surface of MW:BVO was accomplished, resulting in a substantial loading capacity. The modified surface of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs composite is evident through Fe MOF and CQDs treatments. Additionally, Fig. S3 shows the morphology of CQDs in MW:BVO@CQDs electrode to be consistent with that of the prepared CQDs, confirming the successful loading of Fe MOF and CQDs on MW:BVO photonic structure(Fig. 2d). In order to elucidate the chemical composition and distribution of elements on its surface, it was characterized by Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) and Energy Dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX). The analysis results are shown in Fig. 2e and Fig. S4. These results convincingly demonstrate successful loading of Fe MOF and CQDs on MW:BVO surfaces.

Through the utilization of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and EDX, the efficacious modification of MW:BVO with CQDs and Fe MOF was once again validated, as depicted in Fig. 3a and b. TEM analysis confirms the existence of a double-layer coating of Fe MOF and CQDs on the surface of MW:BVO film, which is also supported by the high-resolution TEM images. Fe MOF and CQDs are intimately bound to the MW:BVO surface, demonstrating that the double-layer coating can enhance electron transfer and effectively suppress surface recombination, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance of MW:BVO. As shown in Fig. S5, TEM images also show that the diameter of CQDs is less than 10 nm, and they slightly aggregate, forming a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm that corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite. The distribution of larger octahedral-shaped Fe MOF with a length of approximately 1 μm can be observed in Fig. S6. As shown in Fig. 3c, the HRTEM image of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs confirms that Fe MOF maintains its amorphous structure without any change. Moreover, the lattice spacings of 0.309 and 0.467 nm correspond to the (121) and (011) crystal faces of MW:BVO, respectively. In addition, the lattice spacing of 0.258 nm corresponds to the CQDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that a tight binding between MW:BVO, Fe MOF and CQDs has been realized, which facilitates the charge carrier transport. Furthermore, Fig. 3d and e highlights the uniform distribution of BiVO4 particles (JCPDS No. 14–0688), with an average particle size of approximately 200 nm, the lattice spacings of 0.292 and 0.467 nm correspond to the (040) and (011) crystal faces of MW:BVO, dispersed on the surface of Fe MOF. The utilization of the depicted methodology resulted in highly pure nanohybrids, as supported by the presence of Bi, V, O, Fe and C in the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs catalyst, as revealed by the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum shown in Fig. 3f.

The elemental composition and chemical state of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs composite material surface were analyzed in this study using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS measurement spectra (Fig. S7a) revealed the presence of Bi, V, O, C, N, and Fe elements, confirming the successful deposition of Fe MOF and CQDs on the monoclinic MW:BVO surface, consistent with SEM and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis results. Two peaks were observed in the Bi 4 f high-resolution photoelectron spectroscopy ( Fig. 4a), corresponding to Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2 binding energies, with binding energies of 159.1 and 164.4 eV, respectively [47,48]. High-resolution V 2p photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. 4b) also revealed two peaks corresponding to V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2 binding energies, with binding energies of 516.9 and 524.3 eV, indicating the presence of V5+ in the sample [53]. As shown in Fig. 4c the O 1 s spectra of MW:BVO@Fe MOF and MW:BVO@Fe MOF reveal distinct peaks at 529.5 eV and 531.6 eV, corresponding to the lower binding energy (BE) associated with O2- in the oxygen lattice (OL) and the higher BE associated with OV, respectively. The oxygen in MW:BVO exclusively exists in the OL. The enrichment of OV in Fe MOF and CQDs grown on MW:BVO leads to an increase in the total oxygen content. In addition, the C 1 s photoelectron spectra of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs (Fig. 4d) show three peaks near 293.5, 288.4 and 284.8 eV respectively, corresponding to OC-O, C-O and C-C [54]. CQDs exist on the surface of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs composite.

Fig. S7b illustrates the Fe 2p high-resolution photoelectron spectra of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs. The binding energy of the Fe 2p peaks exhibits negligible changes [55]. Specifically, the peaks observed at 711.9 and 715.5 eV correspond to the 2p3/2 orbital of Fe 2p, while the peak located at 725.6 and 736.4 eV corresponds to th 2p1/2 orbital of Fe 2p [9–13].

A bilayer cocatalyst comprising a layer of Fe MOF and a layer of CQDs was spin-coated on a MW:BVO photocatalyst, followed by photoelectrochemical testing under AM 1.5 G irradiation using a 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous phosphate electrolyte. At a potential of 1.23 V vs. RHE, the BVO electrode manifests a photovoltaic current density of 0.34 mA cm−2 (Fig. S8). As revealed in Fig. 5a, the MW:BVO electrode achieves a considerably higher photovoltaic current density of 0.8 mA cm−2, while the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs electrode exhibited a much higher photocurrent density of 5.8 mA cm−2 at the same potential, which is almost 5 times that of the bare MW:BVO electrode. In order to obtain the best photocatalytic performance, we have carried out in-depth research using various methods. First, we changed the loading of Fe MOF and CQDs to determine the optimal loading of MW:BVO (spin coating 100 μL of Fe MOF and 200 μL of CQDs) and performed the corresponding tests (Fig. S9 and S10). Subsequently, the drying temperature of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs was adjusted, and the optimal drying temperature was determined to be 100 °C. (Fig. S11). Finally, the photocurrent densities of CQDs and Fe MOF at different spin-coating ratios were investigated, and the optimal ratio was determined to be 2:1 (spin-coating 200 μL and 100 μL, respectively) (Fig. S12).

Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) was introduced to characterize the photogenerated carrier complexation of each photoanode (Fig. 5b), and the MW:BVO photoanode showed the lowest fluorescence intensity, which represented a great suppression of its photogenerated carrier complexation, and indicated that the surface loading of CQDs and Fe MOF on the photoanode surface did not make it the center of catalyst complexation. Fig. 5c shows the test of photoanode performance using the electrolyte containing the sacrificial agent Na2SO3, comparing before and after the addition of the sacrificial agent, for the modified photoanode, the measured current gap becomes smaller, which is due to the fact that the double-layer co-catalyst is tightly bonded to the MW:BVO and is sufficiently homogeneous to have the right thickness, which reduces the carrier complex efficiency. Fig. 5d also shows that the current peaks of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs are the most moderate, indicating that the complexation rate is reduced and the photoanode exhibits excellent photocurrent response performance.

In addition, Table 1 lists the PEC performance of the BVO-based photoanode in terms of generating photocurrent density enhancement at 1.23 VRHE. The photocurrent enhancement of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs proposed in this paper is higher than previously reported. The excellent electronic properties of CQDs and the presence of Fe MOF, the improved interfacial charge transfer performance and the reduced surface recombination, therefore, the novel CQDs and Fe MOF are considered to be an efficient cocatalyst for PEC water oxidation.

The optical absorption properties were characterized using UV–vis spectroscopy in Fig. 6a. It is evident that the modification with Fe MOF and CQDs had minimal impact on the absorption edge. To further exploration of the impact on the photoanode band gap of BiVO4 was conducted. The relationship between (αhv)1/2 and hv was graphically depicted to form a Tauc plot (Fig. 6b) using Eq (1). The observed increment in bandgap from 2.52 eV to 2.49 eV with the introduction of Fe MOF and CQDs layers indicates the insignificance of the modified layers' impact on light absorption.

The charge injection efficiency of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode was determined using Eq (2), and the results are presented in Fig. 7a. Evidently, the charge injection efficiency (ηtran) of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode was notably higher than that of the MW:BVO photoanode across the complete potential range, indicating the effective suppression of surface charge recombination by the Fe MOF@CQDs catalyst. As shown in Fig. S13, the charge separation efficiency of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs achieves 91.9%, representing a substantial enhancement in efficiency. It is demonstrated that optimizing the interface contact and employing an appropriate catalyst thickness can significantly enhance the efficiency of surface carrier transfer. Furthermore, the electrocatalytic activity for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) of the respective photoanodes was assessed by recording their polarization curves under complete darkness. It was observed that in the absence of light, the initial potential of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode was lower than that of the MW:BVO(Fig. 7b).The bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) was employed to evaluate the photoelectrochemical performance in the present study, according to the Eq (3). As depicted in Fig. 7c, the maximum ABPE of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode reached 1.2% at 0.95 VRHE which is significantly higher than the maximum ABPE of 0.15% at 1.07 VRHE for MW:BVO photoanode. Therefore, the maximum ABPE of MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQD sphotoanode is nearly 6 times higher than that of MW:BVO photoanode. These results demonstrate that Fe MOF@CQDs composite catalysts can effectively enhance the charge separation in photoelectrochemical systems and facilitate the transport of photo-generated electrons and holes. Fig. 7d displays the inphoton-to-charge conversion efficiency (IPCE), which is an indicator of the monochromatic light's photoelectric conversion efficiency. It is calculated as follows Eq (4) [47,48]. Furthermore, the IPCE value of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanode is very high, reaching 80% at 460 nm, which is more than 5 times that of the MW:BVO photoanode. As shown in Fig. 7e and f, the absorbed photon current efficiency (APCE) is calculated by dividing the IPCE by the light collection efficiency (LHE) for each specific wavelength. As can be seen in Fig. 7f, MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs has the highest photocapture efficiency in the entire visible range. IPCE, APCE and LHE data show that the photoactivity range of BiVO4-based photoanode covers the entire visible spectrum (420–800 nm).

In order to effectively validate the enhanced stability of water oxidation due to the unique structure of the double-layer co-catalyst, current-time (I-t) measurements were conducted. The dissolution of photo-induced V5+ ions from the MW:BVO lattice can lead to severe photo-corrosion phenomena, resulting in a rapid decrease in the photocurrent density of MW:BVO. In comparison to MW:BVO, the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs electrode maintained 92% of its photocurrent after 30 min of irradiation. These findings confirm that the introduction of CQDs and Fe MOF impedes the dissolution of V5+ ions ( Fig. 8a). The open-circuit voltage (OCP) test is employed to assess band bending and charge separation characteristics (Fig. 8b). OCP represents the variation in open-circuit voltage measured under illumination and in the dark; a larger OCP difference indicates greater band bending. A larger OCP is observed on the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanodes compared to MW:BVO, suggesting the formation of greater band bending and higher concentration of OV carriers in MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs. This indicates that the inclusion of CQDs and Fe MOF enhances the exposure of catalytic active sites, thereby promoting surface reactions. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to evaluate the charge transfer capability of the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 8c, the electron transfer resistance (Rct) of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs electrode was lower than that of the bare MW:BVO electrode, indicating the effectiveness of the bilayer cocatalyst in promoting charge separation and transfer. Additionally, Fig. S14 demonstrated that the Rct of the MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs electrode was lower than that of the MW:BVO@CQDs@ Fe MOF electrode, further confirming the suitability of Fe MOF as the outermost cocatalyst to enhance the charge carrier recombination efficiency in BiVO4. The charge recombination behavior of three samples was investigated using the chronoamperometry technique (Fig. 8d and f). The anodic photocurrent peak (Iin) and steady-state photocurrent (Ist) are associated with the accumulation of photogenerated electrons.To accurately assess the behavior of carrier recombination, we performed photocurrent transient testing and introduced a normalized parameter (D) for analysis of Eq (S7). The transient time constant (τ) is an essential parameter reflecting the lifetime of charge carriers, defined as the time at which ln D = −1 in the normalized ln D-t plots. The τ values for the MW:BVO, MW:BVO@CQDs and MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs photoanodes are 2.8, 4.7, and 6.6 s, respectively (Fig. 8e).

Based on the above discussion, a mechanism to improve the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of MW:BVO anode through the synergistic modification of CQDs and Fe MOF is proposed, as shown in Scheme 1. As shown in Fig. 6, the analysis of the result of UV-Vis indicates that the introduction of CQDs does not change the band gap of MW:BVO [56,57], which suggests that the CQDs may not form a heterojunction with MW:BVO. However, electrochemical tests confirm that the incorporation of CQDs reduces the electron transfer resistance between MW:BVO and Fe MOF and significantly improves the charge separation efficiency of the photoexcited carriers, mainly acting as an electronic pathway to improve the separation efficiency of e-/h+ [58]. In addition, the high density of oxygen vacancies introduced by CQDs further enhances the kinetics of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). The Fe MOF coatings not only serve as a protective layer providing active sites for the OER, but also as a synergistic catalyst for the O2 evolution, which improves the charge separation effect [59–61].

The present work introduces a novel strategy for improving the performance of BiVO4 photoanodes by incorporating a dual-layer catalyst. The addition of Fe MOF and CQDs coatings increases the active sites for oxygen evolution, resulting in improved water oxidation activity and photoelectrocatalytic stability. The modified MW:BVO@Fe MOF@CQDs composite photoanode exhibits a remarkable 6-fold increase in photocurrent density compared to the pristine BiVO4 photoanode. The dual-layer catalyst modification surpasses the performance achieved by solely employing Fe MOF modification, offering higher photocurrent density and enhanced photoelectrochemical performance. The Fe MOF and CQDs dual-layer catalyst effectively segregates electrons and holes, enabling efficient photoelectrochemical activity. This study highlights the promising potential of dual-layer catalysts in advancing traditional PEC photoanodes.

Shuai Chu: Validation, Data curation. Wei Zhai: Validation, Data curation. Jie Li: Validation, Data curation. Xiaoru Li: Supervision. zhengbo jiao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Lei Ding: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Lin Wang: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2019MB006), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M632610, 2017M610409).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.174421.
